label: US-relevant macro-financial stress outside NBER recessions
geography: United States with documented global spillovers
scopePolicy: Episodes must not overlap any recession month in nber-us-recessions-v1.json. Labels are multi-dimensional and may overlap each other.

sources.name: BIS Quarterly Review December 2011 - Euro area sovereign crisis drives global financial markets
sources.role: documents September-December 2011 global market stress and flight to quality

sources.name: BIS Quarterly Review September 2015 - EME vulnerabilities take centre stage
sources.role: documents the August 2015 China and emerging-market shock

sources.name: BIS Quarterly Review March 2016 - Uneasy calm gives way to turbulence
sources.role: documents continuation of China, growth and bank stress through February 2016

sources.name: Federal Reserve 2018 Annual Report - Monetary Policy and Economic Developments
sources.role: documents October-December 2018 risk repricing, volatility and tightening financial conditions

sources.name: FRED CPIAUCSL sourced from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics
sources.role: supports the headline CPI year-over-year threshold boundary

sources.name: Federal Reserve Monetary Policy Report June 2022
sources.role: documents broad inflation pressure and tightening financial conditions

sources.name: Federal Reserve Open Market Operations - historical target rate changes
sources.role: dates the March 2022 through July 2023 policy-rate hiking cycle

sources.name: Federal Reserve Financial Stability Report May 2023
sources.role: documents acute banking stress beginning in March 2023 and stabilization by May

taxonomy.label: Financial-system or broad market stress
taxonomy.definition: Documented disruption, flight to quality, material risk repricing or banking-sector stress.

taxonomy.label: Growth scare
taxonomy.definition: Documented material concern about weakening global or US growth without an NBER recession month.

taxonomy.label: Inflation shock
taxonomy.definition: Headline CPI year-over-year at or above 4 percent, supported by institutional assessment of broad price pressure.

taxonomy.label: Rapid monetary tightening
taxonomy.definition: Federal Reserve policy-rate hiking cycle from the first hike through the final hike.

episodes.name: Euro-area sovereign stress spillover
episodes.boundaryRationale: BIS identifies early September through the beginning of December as the period dominated by sovereign-crisis news; the monthly chronology includes September-December.

episodes.name: China and emerging-market growth stress
episodes.boundaryRationale: The August 2015 renminbi and risky-asset shock was followed by renewed global turbulence in January-February 2016; March reporting describes the episode ex post.

episodes.name: Fourth-quarter 2018 risk repricing
episodes.boundaryRationale: The Federal Reserve dates the marked deterioration in risk appetite and decline in risky asset prices from early October through late December 2018.

episodes.name: Post-pandemic inflation shock
episodes.boundaryRationale: Headline CPI year-over-year first reached at least 4 percent in April 2021 and remained at or above 4 percent through May 2023; the Fed documented broad, multi-decade-high inflation pressure.

episodes.name: Federal Reserve rapid tightening cycle
episodes.boundaryRationale: The first target-range increase occurred in March 2022 and the last increase of the cycle occurred in July 2023.

episodes.name: US regional banking stress
episodes.boundaryRationale: Acute bank stress began with March failures; the May Financial Stability Report records normalized markets and stabilized deposit flows while residual stress remained.

limitations: This is a curated ex-post chronology, not an official business-cycle dating system and not a real-time model input.
Episode boundaries combine documented event windows with explicit monthly threshold policies and remain sensitive to the chosen definitions.
Expected regimes are semantic hypotheses fixed before report execution; alignment is descriptive and is not generic classification accuracy.
Only selected US-relevant episodes are included. Absence of a label does not prove absence of stress.
The chronology deliberately excludes NBER recession months and must be cross-validated against the versioned recession ground truth.
